SddSetup.Purpose: Project-level setup. Creates or updates `specs/README.md` (Vision + Scope Graph). Sole owner of the Portal. Idempotent — run any number of times to refine vision, add scopes, or update the graph.
SddSetup.Triggers: - New project from scratch (no specs/README.md). - Operator wants to add a scope to the project graph. - Operator wants to change project-level Vision. - After running discovery for a new scope, operator wants to register it in the Portal.
SddSetup.SkipWhen: - Operator wants to design a specific scope (use discovery). - Operator wants to decompose modules within a scope (use module-decomposition).
SddSetup.Keywords: setup, project init, portal, scope graph, vision, add scope, infra bootstrap
SddSetup.Preconditions: - None: this is the entry point of the SDD flow.

Examples.Positive: - "Start a new project called Mail App." - "Add a mobile scope to the project." - "Update the project vision."
Examples.Negative: - "Design the backend scope." - "Decompose the backend into modules."

SddDiscovery.Purpose: Scope-level discovery session. Creates or evolves a scope spec at `specs/[scope]/[scope].spec.md`. Branches by scope-type: infrastructure (Tool Stack, Workflow, Effective Rules), contracts (Interfaces, Versioning Policy), library (Golden DX, Public API, DbC), product (full flow: Vision, Requirements Gate, Golden DX, Architecture). Living spec — no frozen sections.
SddDiscovery.Triggers: - Operator names a specific scope to design. - Operator describes a raw idea for a scope: new feature, new service, SDK, CLI utility, or app. - Operator asks to "design", "discuss the architecture", "think through how to build" for a scope. - Operator wants to bootstrap infrastructure tooling (scope-type=infrastructure). - scope-type is infrastructure / contracts / library / product. - No scope spec exists for the discussed scope. - Operator wants to refine or pivot an existing scope.
SddDiscovery.SkipWhen: - An approved scope spec already exists and operator wants module decomposition. - Request is a bug fix, performance fix, or local refactor inside an existing module. - Operator asks for a targeted change in existing code without re-evaluating architecture. - Operator wants project-level vision or scope graph changes (use setup).
SddDiscovery.Keywords: discovery, scope, intent, vision, golden DX example, design variants, project type, architecture choice, decision log, requirements gate, raw idea, infrastructure, tooling, contracts, library, scope-type
SddDiscovery.Preconditions: - None for greenfield scope. Portal (specs/README.md) recommended for context but not required.

Examples.Positive: - "I want to design the backend scope." - "Let's design an SDK for event analytics." - "Bootstrap infra-base tooling with pnpm + TypeScript + vitest." - "Design the api-contracts scope for REST v1." - "Refine the web scope to add offline mode."
Examples.Negative: - "Fix a bug in the auth service." - "Add a mobile scope to the project graph." - "Decompose the backend into modules."

SddModuleDecomposition.Purpose: Second scope session. Decompose a product or library scope into module specs at `specs/[scope]/[module]/[module].spec.md`. For each module: enumerate ALL entities (closed-world inventory), describe each entity's public surface, formalize DbC contracts (Ports and Adapters), and design the file structure. Only applicable to scope-type=product and scope-type=library.
SddModuleDecomposition.Triggers: - Discovery session is complete; scope spec at `specs/[scope]/[scope].spec.md` is approved. - Operator moves to module decomposition, entity inventory, or contract formalization. - Operator says "split into modules", "build the module map", "list all entities", "produce DbC", "split Ports and Adapters". - scope-type is product or library.
SddModuleDecomposition.SkipWhen: - Scope spec is missing or not approved. - Discovery is still open, design variants or architecture not yet frozen. - Task tickets already exist — module decomposition belongs earlier in the flow. - scope-type is infrastructure or contracts — those don't decompose into modules. - Scope spec already approved and operator wants task scaffolding.
SddModuleDecomposition.Keywords: module decomposition, module map, hierarchy, entity inventory, closed-world, public surface, consumers, DbC, contracts, Ports, Adapters, file structure, dev rules
SddModuleDecomposition.Preconditions: - An approved scope spec at `specs/[scope]/[scope].spec.md` exists with scope-type=product or library.

Examples.Positive: - "Backend scope is approved, split it into modules." - "Build the entity inventory for the payments module of backend." - "Formalize Ports and Adapters across all modules of the web scope."
Examples.Negative: - "The idea is raw; we still need to discuss what we are building." - "Tasks are already generated; let's start coding." - "Decompose the infra-base scope." (infrastructure — not applicable)

SddTaskScaffolding.Purpose: Third session of the SDD flow. Build a DAG of self-contained task tickets at `tasks/[scope]/[scope].task-NN.md`. Entry point is specs/README.md Scope Graph — canonical map of all scopes and their dependencies. Cascade Table derived from scope graph per-scope. Each ticket MUST explicitly reference relevant spec sections, dependencies, Rule Activation Plan (cascade-derived), BDD scenarios with canonical case names, verification commands, and execution log template.
SddTaskScaffolding.Triggers: - All required scope specs and module specs have contracts and file structures. - Operator asks to decompose specs into tasks. - Operator says "break into tasks", "build the DAG", "produce the execution plan", "scaffold tickets".
SddTaskScaffolding.SkipWhen: - Module specs lack contracts (Ports/Adapters not formalized) for product/library scopes. - Discovery or module decomposition is not yet closed. - Required `coding/[lang]-rules.xml` or `testing/[framework].xml` for the chosen stack is missing. - Tasks are already generated; operator wants a targeted edit to an existing ticket.
SddTaskScaffolding.Keywords: tasks, DAG, decomposition, task tickets, scaffolding, BDD scenarios, execution plan, canonical case names, verification commands, execution log, scope graph, cascade table
SddTaskScaffolding.Preconditions: - `specs/README.md` Scope Graph exists with at least one scope with ✅ status. - `coding/[lang]-rules.xml` exists for every language used in the project. - `testing/[framework].xml` exists for every test runner used in the project.

Examples.Positive: - "All scope specs are ready, break them into tasks." - "Scaffold task tickets for all Adapters in the payments module of backend." - "Build the DAG of execution tasks across the scope graph."
Examples.Negative: - "Architecture is still under discussion." - "There are no contracts yet." - "We don't have coding rules for the target language yet."

SddAudit.Purpose: Post-execution verification session. After a task (or an epic) is executed by an implementation agent, audit compares spec ↔ task ↔ actual code and detects: (1) closed-world drift; (2) completeness gaps; (3) runtime fidelity violations; (4) coding/testing rules compliance violations; (5) insights to backflow into specs. Output: ephemeral session-summary with findings and proposed actions routed to correct artifacts (spec edits, ticket reopens, Decision Log entries). No audit files created.
SddAudit.Triggers: - A task ticket has been executed and its `Execution Log` is filled in. - Operator asks for "audit", "drift detection", "post-implementation review", "verify the implementation". - End of an epic / batch of tasks, before merging or shipping.
SddAudit.SkipWhen: - No executed tasks to audit (Execution Log is empty). - Implementation is still in progress within the same task. - No spec to compare against (audit requires spec ↔ task ↔ code triangulation).
SddAudit.Keywords: audit, post-execution review, drift detection, closed-world fidelity, completeness, runtime fidelity, code review, insight backflow, remediation
SddAudit.Preconditions: - The relevant spec(s) under `specs/` exist. - The task ticket under `tasks/` exists with a non-empty `Execution Log`. - The implementation code referenced by the ticket is committed and accessible.

Examples.Positive: - "Task TSK-12 is done, run the audit." - "Audit the payments epic for drift before we merge." - "Verify that the implementation matches the spec for module X."
Examples.Negative: - "The task is still in progress." - "There's no spec yet, just a prototype."

TypeScriptCodingRules.Purpose: Canonical rules for writing TypeScript code in this project: typing, style, patterns, error handling, anti-patterns, verification hooks.
TypeScriptCodingRules.Triggers: - Execution agent is about to create or modify TypeScript code. - Task ticket declares the implementation language as TypeScript. - Files use `.ts`, `.tsx`, `.mts`, or `.cts` extensions. - Project configuration includes `tsconfig.json` and the task touches compiled modules.
TypeScriptCodingRules.SkipWhen: - Task does not modify TypeScript code (docs only, YAML configs only, shell scripts only). - Target module's stack is a different language (Rust, Python, Go, Swift, Kotlin, etc.).
TypeScriptCodingRules.Keywords: TypeScript, TS, .ts, .tsx, .mts, .cts, interfaces, types, tsc, tsconfig, type narrowing, generics, enum, async/await
TypeScriptCodingRules.Preconditions: - Loaded into the executor's context BEFORE writing or editing TypeScript code. - Task ticket must explicitly reference this file as a mandatory coding rules dependency.

Examples.Positive: - "Implement the PaymentGateway interface in TypeScript." - "Write the OrderProcessor class against the contract from the spec." - "Fix typing in module X."
Examples.Negative: - "Rewrite the deploy script in bash." - "Implement the module in Rust." - "Update README.md."

NodeTestRules.Purpose: Canonical rules for writing tests on the built-in `node:test` runner: file layout, naming, describe/test structure, assertions, mocking, coverage, mapping BDD scenarios to test cases.
NodeTestRules.Triggers: - Execution agent is about to create or modify tests on Node's built-in runner. - Task ticket declares the test framework as `node:test`. - Project's test command is `node --test` (no separate runner like Vitest or Jest). - Test files follow `*.test.ts` / `*.test.js` convention under Node without third-party test runners.
NodeTestRules.SkipWhen: - Tests target a different runner (Vitest, Jest, Mocha, Playwright, Cypress, etc.). - Task does not include writing or modifying tests. - Tests are end-to-end under a browser runner.
NodeTestRules.Keywords: node:test, node --test, test runner, assertions, describe, it, test cases, mock, BDD, canonical case names, coverage
NodeTestRules.Preconditions: - Loaded into the executor's context BEFORE writing tests. - Task ticket must explicitly reference this file as a mandatory testing rules dependency.

Examples.Positive: - "Write `node:test` tests for the OrderProcessor module." - "Cover the BDD scenarios from the ticket with tests on Node's built-in runner." - "Add contract tests for PaymentGatewayPort using `node:test`."
Examples.Negative: - "Write tests using Vitest." - "Build end-to-end tests with Playwright." - "Cover the UI component with Jest snapshots."
